Quick Hits! Writing a computer-assisted story in less than a week

Alison Boggs Graduate student at the University of Missouri School of Journalism October 1992
Demographics & CensusJournalism & Newsroom

Van Denton, a beat reporter for the Raleigh (N.C.) News & Observer, did a routine assignment using a newly formed database and a floppy disk. His paper annually reports on lobbyists' spending on legis...

Van Denton, a beat reporter for the Raleigh (N.C.) News & Observer, did a routine assignment using a newly formed database and a floppy disk. His paper annually reports on lobbyists' spending on legislators, typically involving weeks of combing through documents. Denton found a database at the secretary of state's office, obtained it on a floppy disk, and wrote his story in days. The story detailed lobbyist spending by total, per legislator, and by interest group, like medicine or insurance.

Denton's story ran far ahead of the competition, showcasing how using databases accelerates investigative reporting. Pat Stith from the News & Observer supports Denton's course on computer-assistance, making complex data more accessible to reporters. Similar successes by reporters across the country have shown quick turnaround for deep investigations using databases.
